title: Optics 101
content:
Good clarification to make — these are related but distinct layers of the same optical interconnect stack. Let me search and map it out cleanly.Great question — this is a really important distinction. These three companies sit at **completely different layers** of the same optical interconnect supply chain. Think of it as a vertical stack:

---

## The Optical Interconnect Stack for AI Data Centers

```
LAYER 1 — RAW MATERIALS / SUBSTRATES
         AXTI (AXT Inc.)

LAYER 2 — LASER COMPONENTS / OPTICAL ENGINES
         LITE (Lumentum)

LAYER 3 — SIGNAL PROCESSING / DSP CHIPS
         MXL (MaxLinear)

         ↓ All feed into ↓

     OPTICAL TRANSCEIVERS
  (assembled by module makers)
         ↓
     HYPERSCALER DATA CENTERS
  (Meta, Google, Microsoft, Amazon)
```

---

### 🪨 AXTI — Bottom of the Stack: Raw Substrates

AXTI is a **materials science company**, not a chip or component maker. AXT develops and manufactures high-performance compound semiconductor wafer substrates — indium phosphide (InP), gallium arsenide (GaAs), and germanium (Ge). These substrates are used when a typical silicon wafer cannot meet the performance requirements of a semiconductor or optoelectronic device.

The AI angle: Indium phosphide substrates are a key ingredient in high-speed optical data transmission required in AI-focused data centers. AI data centers are driving a 2.6x increase in 800G+ optical transceiver shipments from ~24M in 2025 to ~63M in 2026 — every one of those transceivers requires InP wafers. AXT is the dominant Western merchant supplier at ~30–35% global share.

The risk: AXT sells bare substrates to epi vendors and laser makers — customers include LandMark, VPEC, WIN Semiconductors, IQE, Lumentum, Coherent, Broadcom, Intel, and MACOM. Heavy China manufacturing exposure means export controls are a live, material risk — in Q3 2025, InP revenues grew 250%+ sequentially to a three-year high as export permits were obtained for significant orders, but permit timing is unpredictable.

---

### 💡 LITE — Middle of the Stack: The Laser/Optical Engine

Lumentum makes the **actual light-emitting components** — specifically EML (electro-absorption modulated laser) chips — that go *inside* the transceiver module. It's the photonic heart of the system.

Inside every high-speed optical transceiver, the EML acts as the core engine — converting electrical data into modulated light signals that travel through fiber. Today, most 400G transceivers use 100Gbps/lane EMLs, but Lumentum leads the next generation with 200Gbps/lane designs powering 800G and 1.6T optics.

Lumentum commands an estimated 50–60% market share in EMLs, placing it at the heart of every 800G and 1.6T optical transceiver deployed in hyperscale data centers. Revenue has exploded: Q2 FY2026 revenue hit $665.5M with a clear path toward $3B in annualized sales.

The landmark AI validation: NVIDIA announced a multi-year strategic agreement with Lumentum including a $2B investment to support R&D, future capacity, and U.S.-based manufacturing in a new fab — with a multi-billion purchase commitment for advanced laser components.

---

### 🧮 MXL — Top of the Stack: Signal Processing / DSP

MaxLinear doesn't make light — it makes the **chip that processes the signal** going into and coming out of the optical transceiver. Its PAM4 DSPs encode/decode the high-speed electrical signals that drive the lasers and interpret the received light.

At the center of MXL's data center momentum is its Keystone PAM4 DSP optical transceiver platform, with infrastructure revenue growing 136% YoY in Q1 2026. The DSP is what allows transceivers to reliably operate at 400G, 800G, and eventually 1.6T speeds.

---

### Side-by-Side Summary

| | **AXTI** | **LITE** | **MXL** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **What they make** | InP/GaAs wafer substrates | EML lasers, optical components | PAM4 DSP signal processor chips |
| **Where in stack** | Raw material (layer 1) | Optical engine (layer 2) | Signal processing (layer 3) |
| **AI driver** | Every transceiver needs InP wafers | Every transceiver needs an EML laser | Every transceiver needs a DSP |
| **Competitive moat** | ~30–35% Western InP share; hard-to-replicate crystal growth | ~50–60% EML share; NVIDIA strategic partner | Keystone 800G incumbent; 1.6T Rushmore ramping |
| **Key risk** | China export controls on InP | Supply constraints on 200G EML capacity | Competition from integrated/captive DSPs |
| **Revenue scale** | ~$88M FY2025 (micro) | ~$2.6B annualized (large cap) | ~$550M annualized (mid cap) |

**Bottom line:** AXTI is the quarry, LITE is the engine factory, and MXL is the electronics brain — all essential, all benefiting from the same AI capex wave, but at very different risk/reward profiles and points of leverage in the value chain.

title: SOXX
content:
Don't buy until SOXX is at 20EMA... But definitely buy then..

Ideally we should avoid huge positions in single stocks , SOXL makes a lot of sense but maybe a flier on a couple of the smaller caps

AXTI, LITE, MXL
